speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Usually, before the war, they were pretty much protective of us. So at least each group had their own little Jew family that was [NON-ENGLISH]. That means 'our Jew.' You don't touch him. You can beat up on everybody else, but not our Jew. And it was true in our case, too. We had-- I know of one particular guy that was pretty hefty and strong. He would really protect us. In many a case, I-- well, one case in particular I remember there was a gang or a group of guys that came in from out of our neighborhood. And they were going to be rambunctious and beat up and, you know, break things up and all that. And this guy came in. And he actually, literally beat up on those guys and chased them away. And he said, you don't touch my little thunder. This is my family, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, right. And so we had a fairly good relationship with our neighboring Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the family's names that you were friendly with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Let's see-- there was Kowalski was the-- the-- what do you call it, the guy that maintained the-- the building and our building. And-- I can't remember. I can't remember names at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the big fella?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't remember at this time either.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- he wasn't so big. He was a short guy, but very strong. And he could pin a guy double his size. For some reason or another, he was strong. Babcaa. Babcaa. B-A-B-C-A-A, I remember that name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he around after the Nazis came, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember seeing him after that. Either he went into the army or what happened. But I didn't see him after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did things start to get worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, before the war, my father happened to remember what-- what happens during the war. So he took all his assets, monies, and purchased goods, you know, dry goods and cigarettes and cans of pickles and things that he knew would be scarce afterwards. And he said money is worthless. Because I remember one scene in our youth where we-- I know I'm mixing up-- but it was a particular Hanukkah. We were baking latkes. And somehow or other we came across a stack of money-- German money, or, you know, before World War I type of money. And we used that to bake latkes with. And this is what reminds me. Because my father says, money isn't worthless during the war. He was-- he had a good-- good brain on him. So he said, whatever money we have, we'll go out and buy goods. And he dry goods. He bought thing that were related to our restaurant and cigarettes and canned foods and things that would not spoil.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long before the Nazis came?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about a month or two months before. We-- we knew it was already bad. Or eve when the war started, yeah. It was right already. Because Warsaw was able to hold up much longer than the rest of the country. Because I understand-- not understand-- I remember the rest of the country was overpowered, overtaken within two weeks. And Warsaw held up for about a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I guess right then and there [INAUDIBLE] I remembered to buy tobacco from the Ministry of Tobacco and Liquor. And we bought as much of cigarettes as we could get and dry goods and all kinds of stuff. And right after-- after they invaded Warsaw, when-- or right when the Germans came in, I remember I had a carton-- not a carton. I had a box with cigarettes that I would sell in the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at one time, I was-- holding the-- you know, selling cigarettes. And-- and I got tapped on the-- on the shoulder. And I looked back. And there was a big German officer with a skull and bones on his-- and I-- I got scared. I didn't know what-- all he wanted to do was buy cigarettes. And that was my first introduction to the German military, the-- the power. And so I gave him the cigarettes with trepidations. I figured, here. Take it. But he paid me. And-- and so why I recall this scene is because we were able to sell the things that we had purchased. And it held up for quite a while. Oh, quite a while, at least the month. Because food became scarce right away. I guess people were hoarding. Either that, or it was burned or whatever. I remember the cans of pickles that we put in our basement in the restaurant. After the restaurant burned down, which also burned down right after the house that we lived in-- the restaurant--

speaker: interviewer
text: Only a couple days after the Nazis came.

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple of days, the-- the restaurant burned down also. And I remember we opened up the basement where the canned goods was. And they were all like exploded, like from the heat. And I was going around We were selling pickles, you know, a nickel a pickle or whatever it was. Because Warsaw was burning, you know, before the-- the invasion. I mean, it was bad. Because the first thing that they-- they-- they somehow knew how to cut off was electricity, water, and all the services. And one of the things I recall that we had to-- I had to run to the Vistula-- Vistula River which runs across Warsaw to get water, just two buckets of water to bring home to drink. And when I came back at one time I was running through like a tunnel of fire where both of the buildings on both sides of the streets were in flame-- in flames and collapsing all around me. And we were running right through in the middle of the street just to bring the two buckets of water to-- to have something to drink. And I know I'm running from one to another because I'm just flashing-- getting flashbacks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's fine. That's fine. So do you remember did the Germans talk about the Jews first when they started to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember that other than the incident with the-- like I said, when they throwing out the bread for the propaganda purposes to show that they're feeding the crowds. And the incident with the Jew being cut off the beard on one side. And-- but by that time-- oh, let me backtrack a little. Because when our house burned down, we took whatever we dug up from the basement and made bundles. And each one had a preassigned bundle to-- to grab in case we had to run. And my father, [NON-ENGLISH], arranged with my brother's fiancee's sister, who lived on [INAUDIBLE] Street, which is in the Jewish section in a big building. And that building was protected by the military, because they-- I think they had the headquarters there. Because they had the anti-aircraft guns on the roof. And so that building was still standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they arranged with the military to send a soldier with a wagon to take our belongings to bring us over there from [INAUDIBLE] Street to [INAUDIBLE]. And I was elected to go with the wagon. And it was a big wagon with one or two horses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guy was so scared. He was literally flying through the air. And I was holding on. And we were going right through the-- all the burning streets from [INAUDIBLE] to [INAUDIBLE] Street. And when we got there, we quickly unloaded and got into that building. It was tremendous overcrowded already, because people were coming from all over the burning parts of the city. And so we stayed with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of wagon was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was one of those long-- long wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was motorized?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a military-- no, no, no. With-- with horses. And-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, the typical Polish type of long wagons. I don't know how to describe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many cars or mostly horses in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were cars. There were cars before the war. As a matter of fact, one of my cousins was a taxi driver. And before the war, I remember when he came by. And it was a big thrill for me to-- when he gave me a ride for a couple of blocks. And then I had to run home. But I ran. You know, I was happy to be in a taxi. And that was a highlight. But so at that time, we knew that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things are bad and worse. So I mean, it was-- a lot was destroyed. And when the-- finally, when the army came in, the German army, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were not right away bad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a lot of confusion. But we sort of settled down for a little while. I don't really recall much of that period. Of course, there was no-- no schools, no business. I'm trying to remember what we did to earn a living even at that time. I guess we were just selling off the items that my father bought before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that illegal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it legal?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think there was any questions. There wasn't that much of authority really. The military was pretty much governing-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] quite frankly, I don't remember what-- what kind of a political situation was there. And at that point-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, before-- let me backtrack a little bit to give us a little relationship between my brothers and-- and I. My oldest brother, George was in the Polish army and was called up before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was-- he was born in 1913. So he was 26. Yeah. So he was in the Polish army. And he-- I know he was in the anti-aircraft lights-- what do you call it-- the [NON-ENGLISH]. And my other brother, Jacob, was of military age already. But they didn't-- I don't think they had enough uniforms for them. And they were ordered to just to get out of Warsaw and more or less follow the army wherever they went. And-- the Polish army kept retreating towards the Russian border. And they-- they were either go to Romania or something like that. They-- they kept retreating. And at that time, the Russians decided to come in and occupy the eastern part of Poland. And by that time, they overtook the-- the Polish army. And I know my brother said he decided at that time to just take off the uniforms. They disbanded. There was really chaos there. And he became a civilian under the Russian occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ever come back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He didn't. But my other brother, Jacob-- Yakob, we used to call him-- who was told to follow the army, also wound up under the Russian occupation, so to speak. And somehow, I don't know exactly how it happened, but they met up, the two of them. And George, my oldest brother said to Jack, you know, things I hear are bad in Warsaw. We got to do something for the family. So Jack said, OK, I'll go back and bring him-- bring him out. And so he came back to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In October, I guess. September, October, yeah, just about a month after the-- after the Germans took over Poland. And he came back, naturally smuggled the border, and came back. And he told us that he met up with George. And they want us to come to join them. And my father, by that time, knew that things were not going to get any better. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he know?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from the situation, the way he saw the might and the power of the German army and that. So in any case, decided to follow my brother, Jacob, to go to occupied Poland to the Russian side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. Yeah. Because I-- well, I really didn't have that much of an opinion, you know, being a 15-year-old. So I-- so we decided to split up the family, so we wouldn't go with one group.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother, Jacob, and I and a couple of the other boys from our neighborhood joined him. And somehow they rented a truck and were able to bring us to the border, which was the Bug River, the B-U-G, which was the borderline between Russia and Germany at the time. Where-- what's the-- what town was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in [INAUDIBLE]. And I don't know exactly-- well, where we wound up going to was the town of Krzemieniec. K-R-Z-E-M-E-N-I-E-C. Krzemieniec. And in Russian they changed it to [RUSSIAN WORD] without the z's. And so we wound up-- I remember going with the-- with the truck up to the river. And at that time, they arranged to smuggle across by boat, by a little boat, a row boat. And I remember I was scared, because I never knew how to swim. And water is not my favorite sport. So I-- I was in the bottom of the-- of the boat when everyone is rowing quietly during the night, just pedaling across quietly. And when we got to the other side, my brother told everybody-- I guess that was the pre-arranged-- that if we meet up with the Russian border patrol to tell them that we are on the way to Germany. So he said they would prevent us from going to Germany. And so it happens. We did wind up meeting up a Russian patrol. And we all kept saying, we're going to Germany, we're Poland. And he said, can't go it. Go back. Go back, sending us back in to Russia. And that, of course, was the whole plan to do it. And they had-- they knew that that was the prearranged agreement. And so we wound up on the Russian side. And we went to Krzemieniec or [INAUDIBLE]. And I met my brother, George, who already arranged to have an apartment for us. And he was a good looking guy, my brother, George. And a lot of women were after him. And they would do anything just to make a [INAUDIBLE] for him to marry their daughters and whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even then. Oh, yeah. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: --he managed to get us this beautiful apartment up on a hill in the-- in the town of Krzemieniec. And in the meantime, my brother-- and he didn't go back yet. But he another group arranged to bring my parents, and my sister, and my brother, Sam, and his fiancee, Shaindl. To bring us back to Krzemieniec. And sure enough, within a few days, they arrived over there. And we all settled down. In the meantime, my brother, Sam, got sick. And it was the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it wasn't the cholera-- a very serious disease. And he wound up in the hospital in Krzemieniec. And thank God, he got well, because of the good arrangements that we had. So the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did people know you were Jewish in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. It was a very, very Jewish organized community in Krzemieniec. And the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when we got there, we all got organized. And my father started-- got a job in the restaurant in town, being that he was a restaurateur. And my brother, George, the oldest one being-- he was an electrician in his trade. So he got work right away. And my brother, Sam, who happens to be very artistic and-- well, a sign painter by trade-- but he also used to like to paint pictures. And I remember he painted a big portrait of Stalin. And that way he got ingratiated in the political hierarchy. And they gave him another-- also a job in the same restaurant. And I wound up going to school. And it was a nice town. They sent me-- it was originally an ORT school-- a trade school. Operated by ORT, but then it was taken over by the government. It was an auto mechanic school. And they were paying me money to go to school. And they had a stipend. And the reason for that is they needed auto mechanics in their collective farms. And I had to finish school, and then be allowed to be assigned to a-- to a position that they would choose and be an auto mechanic-- a mechanic for the autos and tractors. Which, at the time, I never gave any thought about it as long as we got a good education plus a stipend, which was-- I don't remember-- all I remember big-- big ruble bills. And so we had a fairly-- fairly nice life there for a few weeks or month or whatever. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was everybody starting to become communist there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was communists. There was-- communism was the big thing. And the-- the thing that-- that got bad is the fact that my brother Jacob went back to Poland to bring back more people, to rescue more people. And unfortunately, he got caught by the Russians on the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had lead us with him, you know, to the families in Poland. And they accused him of being a spy.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was when he was trying to go back to--?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he was trying to go back to Poland to bring back more family, family and friends. And they-- and they-- at that time, we didn't know it. But I've-- we've since found out that he was sentenced as a spy and sent to Archangelsk, which is up north, the gold mines of Russia. Archangelsk, I think is A-R-C-H-A-N-G-E-L-S-K, Archangelsk. That's a very-- a terrible prison place, labor-- labor camp. And unfortunately, he-- he did not survive. We-- we didn't know about it. Well, we heard that he was sentenced and sent there. But we didn't know what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew that he'd been picked up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. From other people, we saw that he was picked up. And they followed the-- the tribunal or whatever the court was. And we didn't know anything about after that until--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was right in November-- '39-- no, it was, in-- yeah, November of '39, right after we got to the Ukraine. And-- but after the war, I did a lot of search through Red Cross. And we couldn't find out. It's only recently when the Russians opened up their-- just a  year ago or so, two years, the Russians opened up their archives. And they kept records of their prisoners similar to what the Germans had, I guess. And I got a letter from the Red Cross-- from the Russian equivalent-- with the date that he actually died. Because before that, we knew that he perished. We didn't know when. So the-- although, my mother shall refused to believe that he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she never wanted to talk about it. He's still alive. Someday he'll show up. But after she passed away, my brothers and I and my sister decided we have to establish a yahrzeit for Jack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we all did. We made up a yahrzeit of the last day that we all saw him alive. And that's how we commemorated a day for him. And after I got the letter from them, they actually gave me the date of December 1940 that he died. He was 24 years old. And so we have a different yahrzeit for him. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what happened? How did the family react to all of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we naturally were sad. The-- but this was after the fact. I mean, we had no way of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. I'm saying at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: --contacting or knowing where he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all through the years when our parents were still alive we thought maybe he survived being a young man. But we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. In Krzemieniec, we stayed [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] til about September 1940, which is almost a year there. But before that, my brother, George-- who prior to the war, before the war, had plans to go to America. But during that-- at the time before the war, he was about to get a visa to-- to go to America. But because of the influx of refugees from Germany, Germany expelled all the so-called Polish citizens, the Jewish citizens, back to Poland. And so they set back his visa to give them priority, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Polish Jews from Germany. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But he had some documents, some papers, that showed that he was on a waiting list.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so he tried to get a visa again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he tried to get a visa. He couldn't get a visa. But he found out that in Lithuania, in-- in Wilno, there was a Polish-- not the Polish, the American Embassy [INAUDIBLE] still had an office there. So he went to-- to Wilno, which is-- was also occupied by the Russians. And he was able to get there.
